title: Batch covariate retrieval in getOutputValues for population simulations
body:
## Issue Overview

Optimize nested loops with .NET interop calls in `getOutputValues` that currently make O(n*m) .NET calls where n=number of individuals and m=number of covariates.

## Current Problem

**File**: `R/utilities-simulation-results.R:74-91`

For a population of 1000 individuals with 10 covariates, this results in **10,000 .NET calls**:

```r
for (individualIndex in seq_along(individualIds)) {
  individualId <- individualIds[individualIndex]
  individualProperties <- list(IndividualId = rep(individualId, valueLength))

  for (covariateName in covariateNames) {  # NESTED LOOP
    covariateValue <- population$getCovariateValue(
      covariateName,
      individualId
    )  # .NET CALL PER INDIVIDUAL PER COVARIATE
    individualProperties[[covariateName]] <- rep(covariateValue, valueLength)
  }
}
```

## Proposed Solution

Implement batch retrieval of all covariate values:

```r
# Option 1: Batch retrieve all covariates for all individuals
if (!is.null(population)) {
  # Get all covariate values at once using vectorized .NET call
  allCovariateValues <- population$getAllCovariateValues(individualIds)

  for (individualIndex in seq_along(individualIds)) {
    individualId <- individualIds[individualIndex]
    individualProperties <- list(IndividualId = rep(individualId, valueLength))

    for (covariateName in covariateNames) {
      covariateValue <- allCovariateValues[[covariateName]][individualIndex]
      individualProperties[[covariateName]] <- rep(covariateValue, valueLength)
    }

    individualPropertiesCache[[individualIndex]] <- individualProperties
  }
}
```

## Expected Impact

- **Priority**: CRITICAL
- **Estimated Impact**: 50-80% reduction in result extraction time for population simulations
- **Effort**: Medium
- **Testing**: Benchmark with populations of 100, 1000, 10000 individuals

## Implementation Notes

- May require exposing batch .NET API for getting all covariate values
- This is in the hot path of all population simulation result extraction
- Create benchmark before and after implementation

## Parent Issue

This is part of the comprehensive performance optimization analysis in #1765

title: Optimize unit conversion operations to reduce .NET interop calls
body:
## Issue Overview

Reduce multiple .NET calls per unit conversion operation. Currently up to 4 .NET calls per `toUnit()` invocation.

## Current Problem

**File**: `R/utilities-units.R:148-242`

Benchmark evidence shows significant overhead:
- **70k ms** for 5000 iterations with 100k values
- **Memory usage**: 14,753 MB indicating heavy allocations
- Existing comments show "New version: 7k ms, 2290 mb" vs old "40k ms, 10556 mb" - indicating 82% improvement is possible

```r
toUnit <- function(
  quantityOrDimension,
  values,
  targetUnit,
  sourceUnit = NULL,
  molWeight = NULL,
  molWeightUnit = NULL
) {
  # Up to 4 separate .NET calls:
  # 1. Convert molWeight to base unit
  # 2. Convert values to base unit
  # 3. Convert to target unit
  # Plus unnecessary as.numeric(c(values)) creating copies
}
```

## Proposed Solution

1. Add early returns for same-unit conversions
2. Eliminate unnecessary `as.numeric(c(values))` copies
3. Check if source and target are the same BEFORE any .NET calls
4. Combine multiple .NET calls where possible

```r
toUnit <- function(
  quantityOrDimension,
  values,
  targetUnit,
  sourceUnit = NULL,
  molWeight = NULL,
  molWeightUnit = NULL
) {
  # Early return for same unit without source
  if (is.null(sourceUnit) && targetUnit == baseUnit) {
    return(values)
  }

  # Avoid unnecessary memory allocation if values are already numeric
  if (!is.numeric(values)) {
    values <- as.numeric(values)
  }

  # Check if source and target are the same BEFORE any .NET calls
  if (!is.null(sourceUnit) && sourceUnit == targetUnit) {
    return(values)
  }

  # Minimize .NET calls...
}
```

## Expected Impact

- **Priority**: CRITICAL
- **Estimated Impact**: 50-80% reduction in unit conversion time (evidence from existing benchmark)
- **Effort**: Medium-High
- **Testing**: Run existing benchmark script `tests/dev/script-benchmark-unitConversion.R` to verify improvements

## Evidence

From `tests/dev/script-benchmark-unitConversion.R`:
- Same unit conversion: 40+ ms per 1000 iterations
- Comments show 82% improvement is achievable with optimization

## Parent Issue

This is part of the comprehensive performance optimization analysis in #1765

title: Replace rbind.data.frame with data.table for efficient row binding
body:
## Issue Overview

Replace inefficient `rbind.data.frame` operations with `data.table::rbindlist` for binding many small data frames in population simulation result processing.

## Current Problem

**File**: `R/utilities-simulation-results.R:94-97`

```r
allIndividualProperties <- do.call(
  rbind.data.frame,
  c(individualPropertiesCache, stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
)
```

**Issues**:
- `rbind.data.frame` is slow for binding many small data frames
- Creates intermediate copies during the binding process
- For 1000 individuals: combines 1000 separate list structures into one data frame
- Potential O(n²) complexity in worst case due to repeated memory allocations

## Proposed Solution

**Option 1**: Use data.table for efficient row binding

```r
library(data.table)
allIndividualProperties <- data.table::rbindlist(
  individualPropertiesCache,
  use.names = TRUE
)
```

**Option 2**: Pre-allocate and fill by columns

```r
nRows <- length(individualIds) * valueLength
allIndividualProperties <- data.frame(
  IndividualId = integer(nRows),
  Time = numeric(nRows),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)
# Pre-allocate columns for each covariate
for (covariateName in covariateNames) {
  allIndividualProperties[[covariateName]] <- numeric(nRows)
}
# Fill in values more efficiently
rowIdx <- 1
for (individualIndex in seq_along(individualIds)) {
  endIdx <- rowIdx + valueLength - 1
  allIndividualProperties$IndividualId[rowIdx:endIdx] <- individualIds[individualIndex]
  # ... fill other columns
  rowIdx <- endIdx + 1
}
```

## Expected Impact

- **Priority**: HIGH
- **Estimated Impact**: 40-60% reduction in data frame construction time
- **Effort**: Low
- **Testing**: Benchmark with various population sizes (100, 1000, 10000 individuals)

## Implementation Notes

- Affects all population simulation result processing
- Verify that data.table is available as a dependency or add it
- Ensure backward compatibility of output format

## Parent Issue

This is part of the comprehensive performance optimization analysis in #1765

title: Implement batch entity matching to reduce .NET interop calls
body:
## Issue Overview

Reduce repeated entity matching .NET calls by implementing batch retrieval for multiple entity paths.

## Current Problem

**File**: `R/utilities-entity.R:116-143`

```r
.getAllEntitiesMatching <- function(
  paths,
  container,
  entityType,
  method = NULL
) {
  task <- .getNetTaskFromCache("ContainerTask")
  method <- method %||% AllMatchingMethod[[className]]

  findEntitiesByPath <- function(path) {
    .toObjectType(task$call(method, container, path), entityType)  # .NET CALL PER PATH
  }

  return(.unify(findEntitiesByPath, paths))
}
```

**Issues**:
- For n paths: n separate .NET calls to ContainerTask
- For 100 paths = 100 .NET interop calls
- No batching of entity retrieval

## Proposed Solution

Implement batch entity retrieval:

```r
.getAllEntitiesMatching <- function(
  paths,
  container,
  entityType,
  method = NULL
) {
  if (length(paths) == 0) {
    return(list())
  }

  task <- .getNetTaskFromCache("ContainerTask")
  method <- method %||% AllMatchingMethod[[className]]

  # Option 1: Single batched .NET call if API supports it
  entities <- task$call(
    paste0(method, "Batch"),  # e.g., "GetAllQuantitiesMatchingBatch"
    container,
    paths
  )
  return(.toObjectType(entities, entityType))
}
```

## Expected Impact

- **Priority**: HIGH
- **Estimated Impact**: 50-70% reduction in entity lookup time
- **Effort**: Medium
- **Testing**: Test with multiple entity types and varying path counts (10, 50, 100 paths)

## Implementation Notes

- May require implementing batch .NET API methods
- Affects all entity lookup operations throughout the package
- Fallback to individual calls if batch API not available

## Parent Issue

This is part of the comprehensive performance optimization analysis in #1765

title: Reduce multiple split/apply/bind cycles in unit conversion
body:
## Issue Overview

Consolidate multiple split/apply/bind cycles in data unit conversion operations that currently iterate over the entire dataset multiple times.

## Current Problem

**File**: `R/utilities-data-combined.R:636-662`

```r
# Split 1 for xUnit conversion
xDataList <- .removeEmptyDataFrame(split(data, data$xUnitSplit))
data <- purrr::map_dfr(
  .x = xDataList,
  .f = function(data) .xUnitConverter(data, xTargetUnit)
)

# Split 2 for yUnit conversion
yDataList <- .removeEmptyDataFrame(split(
  data,
  list(data$yUnitSplit, data$molWeightSplit)
))
data <- purrr::map_dfr(
  .x = yDataList,
  .f = function(data) .yUnitConverter(data, yTargetUnit)
)

# Split 3 for yError conversion
if (any(colnames(data) == "yErrorValues")) {
  yErrorDataList <- .removeEmptyDataFrame(split(
    data,
    list(data$yErrorUnitSplit, data$molWeightSplit)
  ))
  data <- purrr::map_dfr(
    .x = yErrorDataList,
    .f = function(data) .yErrorUnitConverter(data, yTargetUnit)
  )
}
```

**Issues**:
- Three separate `split()` operations on potentially large data frames
- Multiple `purrr::map_dfr()` calls involve repeated row binding
- Each split/apply/bind cycle iterates over the entire dataset
- High overhead from data frame reorganization
- O(n*k) complexity where n=rows, k=number of conversions

## Proposed Solution

Process by groups using data.table for efficiency:

```r
library(data.table)
data <- as.data.table(data)

# Single pass conversion
data[, `:=`(
  xValues = .xUnitConverter(.SD, xTargetUnit),
  yValues = .yUnitConverter(.SD, yTargetUnit)
), by = .(xUnitSplit, yUnitSplit, molWeightSplit)]
```

## Expected Impact

- **Priority**: HIGH
- **Estimated Impact**: 40-60% reduction in data processing time for unit conversions
- **Effort**: Medium
- **Testing**: Test with various unit combinations and dataset sizes

## Implementation Notes

- Affects all data processing with unit conversions
- Consider using data.table for efficient grouped operations
- Maintain backward compatibility of output format

## Parent Issue

This is part of the comprehensive performance optimization analysis in #1765

title: Optimize sequential simulation addition in batch operations
body:
## Issue Overview

Optimize the sequential addition of simulations in `runSimulationsConcurrently` to reduce .NET interop overhead.

## Current Problem

**File**: `R/utilities-simulation.R:315-340`

```r
for (simulationIdx in seq_along(simulations)) {
  simulation <- simulations[[simulationIdx]]
  simulationIdSimulationMap[[simulationIdx]] <- simulation
  names(simulationIdSimulationMap)[[simulationIdx]] <- simulation$id

  simulationRunner$call("AddSimulation", simulation)  # .NET CALL PER SIMULATION
}
```

**Issues**:
- n separate .NET calls to AddSimulation before RunConcurrently
- For 100 simulations = 100 separate .NET interop calls
- Sequential building of simulationIdSimulationMap with repeated name assignments

## Proposed Solution

**Option 1**: Batch addition if .NET API supports it

```r
simulationIds <- sapply(simulations, function(sim) sim$id)
simulationIdSimulationMap <- setNames(simulations, simulationIds)
simulationRunner$call("AddSimulations", simulations)  # Single .NET call
```

**Option 2**: Optimize R-side processing if individual calls required

```r
simulationIdSimulationMap <- vector("list", length(simulations))
names(simulationIdSimulationMap) <- sapply(simulations, function(sim) sim$id)

for (simulationIdx in seq_along(simulations)) {
  simulation <- simulations[[simulationIdx]]
  simulationIdSimulationMap[[simulationIdx]] <- simulation
  simulationRunner$call("AddSimulation", simulation)
}
```

## Expected Impact

- **Priority**: MEDIUM-HIGH
- **Estimated Impact**: 20-30% reduction in batch simulation setup time
- **Effort**: Low-Medium
- **Testing**: Benchmark with 10, 50, 100 simulations

## Implementation Notes

- Affects batch simulation initialization time
- Check if .NET API supports batch addition
- Optimize R-side map building regardless

## Parent Issue

This is part of the comprehensive performance optimization analysis in #1765

title: Implement batch metadata retrieval for simulation results
body:
## Issue Overview

Reduce multiple .NET calls for metadata retrieval by implementing batch operations for unit and dimension information.

## Current Problem

**File**: `R/utilities-simulation-results.R:104-130`

```r
if (addMetaData) {
  metaData <- lapply(paths, function(path) {
    unit <- NULL
    dimension <- NULL
    if (!all(is.na(values[[path]]))) {
      unit <- task$call(
        "BaseUnitNameByPath",  # .NET CALL 1
        simulationResults$simulation,
        path,
        stopIfNotFound
      )
      dimension <- task$call(
        "DimensionNameByPath",  # .NET CALL 2
        simulationResults$simulation,
        path,
        stopIfNotFound
      )
    }
    list(unit = unit, dimension = dimension)
  })
  names(metaData) <- paths
}
```

**Issues**:
- 2 .NET calls per path for metadata
- For 50 output paths = 100 .NET calls
- Sequential processing

## Proposed Solution

Batch retrieve metadata:

```r
if (addMetaData) {
  # Filter paths with valid values first
  validPaths <- paths[!vapply(values, function(v) all(is.na(v)), logical(1))]

  # Batch retrieve metadata if API supports it
  if (length(validPaths) > 0) {
    metadataList <- task$call(
      "GetMetadataForPaths",  # Hypothetical batch method
      simulationResults$simulation,
      validPaths,
      stopIfNotFound
    )
  }

  # Process results...
}
```

## Expected Impact

- **Priority**: MEDIUM-HIGH
- **Estimated Impact**: 40-60% reduction in metadata retrieval time
- **Effort**: Medium
- **Testing**: Test with varying numbers of output paths (10, 50, 100)

## Implementation Notes

- Affects result extraction when metadata is needed
- May require .NET API enhancement for batch metadata retrieval
- Filter valid paths before processing to reduce calls

## Parent Issue

This is part of the comprehensive performance optimization analysis in #1765

title: Cache population count to reduce property access overhead
body:
## Issue Overview

Cache the population count property to avoid repeated .NET calls during parameter value setting operations.

## Current Problem

**File**: `R/population.R:52-65`

```r
setParameterValues = function(parameterOrPath, values) {
  parameterPath <- private$getPathFrom(parameterOrPath)
  validateIsNumeric(values)

  expectedCount <- self$count  # Property access - potential .NET call
  actualCount <- length(values)
  if (actualCount != expectedCount) {
    stop(...)
  }

  self$call("SetValues", parameterPath, values)
}
```

**Issues**:
- `self$count` may trigger .NET interop if not cached
- Called frequently during parameter value setting operations
- Validation overhead on every call

## Proposed Solution

Cache count in private field:

```r
setParameterValues = function(parameterOrPath, values) {
  parameterPath <- private$getPathFrom(parameterOrPath)
  validateIsNumeric(values)

  # Use cached count if available
  if (is.null(private$.cachedCount)) {
    private$.cachedCount <- self$count
  }
  expectedCount <- private$.cachedCount

  actualCount <- length(values)
  if (actualCount != expectedCount) {
    stop(messages$errorWrongNumberOfValues(actualCount, expectedCount))
  }

  self$call("SetValues", parameterPath, values)
}
```

## Expected Impact

- **Priority**: MEDIUM
- **Estimated Impact**: 10-15% reduction in parameter setting overhead
- **Effort**: Low
- **Testing**: Test parameter setting operations with various population sizes

## Implementation Notes

- Affects parameter setting operations
- Initialize cache during object construction or first access
- Ensure cache is invalidated if population count can change

## Parent Issue

This is part of the comprehensive performance optimization analysis in #1765

title: Optimize entity to path conversion with vectorization
Implement batch quantity value setting
body:
## Issue Overview

Optimize quantity value setting by batching unit conversions and property assignments.

## Current Problem

**File**: `R/utilities-quantity.R:109-120`

```r
for (i in seq_along(quantities)) {
  quantity <- quantities[[i]]
  value <- values[[i]]
  if (!is.null(units)) {
    value <- toBaseUnit(
      quantityOrDimension = quantity,
      values = value,
      unit = units[[i]]
    )  # Per-iteration .NET call
  }
  quantity$value <- value  # Property assignment per iteration
}
```

**Issues**:
- For each of n quantities: 1 toBaseUnit call + 1 property assignment
- For 100 quantities = potentially 100+ .NET calls
- No batching of unit conversions or property assignments

## Proposed Solution

Batch convert units for quantities with the same dimension:

```r
if (!is.null(units)) {
  # Group quantities by dimension for batch conversion
  dimensionGroups <- split(
    seq_along(quantities),
    vapply(quantities, function(q) q$dimension, character(1))
  )

  convertedValues <- values
  for (dimGroup in dimensionGroups) {
    if (length(dimGroup) > 1) {
      # Batch conversion for same dimension
      convertedValues[dimGroup] <- toBaseUnit(
        quantityOrDimension = quantities[[dimGroup[1]]],
        values = values[dimGroup],
        unit = units[dimGroup]
      )
    } else {
      idx <- dimGroup[1]
      convertedValues[idx] <- toBaseUnit(
        quantityOrDimension = quantities[[idx]],
        values = values[[idx]],
        unit = units[[idx]]
      )
    }
  }
  values <- convertedValues
}

# Set all values
for (i in seq_along(quantities)) {
  quantities[[i]]$value <- values[[i]]
}
```

## Expected Impact

- **Priority**: MEDIUM
- **Estimated Impact**: 30-40% reduction in quantity value setting time
- **Effort**: Medium
- **Testing**: Test with quantities of various dimensions

## Implementation Notes

- Affects quantity value setting operations
- Group by dimension for batch conversion opportunities
- Consider batch .NET API for setting multiple values if available

## Parent Issue

This is part of the comprehensive performance optimization analysis in #1765

title: Implement automatic property access caching in DotNetWrapper
Optimize unique entity operations with faster duplicate detection
Optimize cache operations for individual simulation results
Reduce unnecessary memory copies in vector operations
body:
## Issue Overview

Eliminate unnecessary vector copies created by redundant type conversion operations.

## Current Problem

**Multiple Files**

```r
# Pattern seen in multiple places
values <- as.numeric(c(values))  # Unnecessary copy if already numeric vector

# In utilities-units.R:242
dimensionTask <- .getNetTaskFromCache("DimensionTask")
values <- as.numeric(c(values))  # Creates unnecessary copies
```

**Issues**:
- `c(values)` creates a copy even if values is already a vector
- `as.numeric()` on numeric values creates another copy
- For large value arrays (100k elements): unnecessary memory allocation

## Proposed Solution

Check type before conversion:

```r
# Check type before conversion
if (!is.numeric(values)) {
  values <- as.numeric(values)
} else if (is.list(values)) {
  values <- as.numeric(unlist(values))
}

# Or use more efficient coercion
values <- as.double(values)  # Faster than as.numeric for vectors
```

## Expected Impact

- **Priority**: MEDIUM
- **Estimated Impact**: 15-25% reduction in memory allocations
- **Effort**: Low
- **Testing**: Test with large value arrays and monitor memory usage

## Implementation Notes

- Memory overhead in unit conversions and data processing
- Scan codebase for this pattern in multiple files
- Use `as.double()` instead of `as.numeric()` for better performance

## Parent Issue

This is part of the comprehensive performance optimization analysis in #1765

title: Remove unnecessary column creation in unit converter
body:
## Issue Overview

Optimize memory usage by removing temporary split columns in unit conversion operations.

## Current Problem

**File**: `R/utilities-units.R:529-681`

```r
.unitConverter <- function(data, xUnit = NULL, yUnit = NULL) {
  # Early return (good)
  if (
    length(unique(data$xUnit)) == 1L &
      is.null(xUnit) &
      length(unique(data$yUnit)) == 1L &
      is.null(yUnit)
  ) {
    return(data)
  }

  # But then STILL creates Split columns even if not needed
  data <- dplyr::mutate(
    data,
    dplyr::across(
      .cols = dplyr::matches("Unit$|Weight$"),
      .fns = as.character,
      .names = "{.col}Split"
    )
  )
}
```

**Issues**:
- Added columns (xUnitSplit, yUnitSplit, molWeightSplit) for split operations
- Increases memory footprint: 1000 rows × 5 new columns = 5000 additional cells
- Columns persist through multiple operations
- Early return doesn't prevent column creation in later code paths

## Proposed Solution

Create split columns only when needed and remove them before returning:

```r
.unitConverter <- function(data, xUnit = NULL, yUnit = NULL) {
  # Enhanced early return
  if (
    length(unique(data$xUnit)) == 1L &
      is.null(xUnit) &
      length(unique(data$yUnit)) == 1L &
      is.null(yUnit)
  ) {
    return(data)
  }

  # Create split columns in-place only when needed
  # Use data.table for efficient in-place operations
  dt <- data.table::as.data.table(data)

  # Only add columns that don't exist
  if (!"xUnitSplit" %in% names(dt)) {
    dt[, xUnitSplit := as.character(xUnit)]
  }

  # Process conversions
  # ...

  # Remove temporary split columns before returning
  dt[, c("xUnitSplit", "yUnitSplit", "molWeightSplit") := NULL]

  return(as.data.frame(dt))
}
```

## Expected Impact

- **Priority**: MEDIUM
- **Estimated Impact**: 15-25% reduction in memory usage for large datasets
- **Effort**: Medium
- **Testing**: Test with various dataset sizes and monitor memory usage

## Implementation Notes

- Memory overhead for large datasets
- Use data.table for efficient in-place column operations
- Clean up temporary columns before returning

## Parent Issue

This is part of the comprehensive performance optimization analysis in #1765

title: Cache path separator pattern in path operations
body:
## Issue Overview

Optimize path string operations by caching the split pattern instead of recreating it on every call.

## Current Problem

**File**: `R/utilities-path.R:12-34`

```r
toPathArray <- function(path) {
  validateIsString(path)
  unlist(
    strsplit(path, paste0("\\", ospsuiteEnv$pathSeparator)),
    use.names = FALSE
  )
}

toPathString <- function(...) {
  pathStrings <- c(...)
  validateIsString(pathStrings)
  paste(pathStrings, collapse = ospsuiteEnv$pathSeparator)
}
```

**Issues**:
- `strsplit` with regex pattern escaping for each path
- Pattern `paste0("\\", ospsuiteEnv$pathSeparator)` recreated each time
- If called 1000 times: 1000 pattern creations + string operations

## Proposed Solution

Cache the split pattern:

```r
# Cache the split pattern
.splitPattern <- NULL
.getSplitPattern <- function() {
  if (is.null(.splitPattern)) {
    .splitPattern <<- paste0("\\", ospsuiteEnv$pathSeparator)
  }
  .splitPattern
}

toPathArray <- function(path) {
  validateIsString(path)
  unlist(
    strsplit(path, .getSplitPattern(), fixed = FALSE),
    use.names = FALSE
  )
}

# For better performance with fixed = TRUE if no regex needed
toPathArray <- function(path) {
  validateIsString(path)
  unlist(
    strsplit(path, ospsuiteEnv$pathSeparator, fixed = TRUE),
    use.names = FALSE
  )
}
```

## Expected Impact

- **Priority**: LOW-MEDIUM
- **Estimated Impact**: 15-25% reduction in path string operations
- **Effort**: Low
- **Testing**: Test with various path operations

## Implementation Notes

- Called frequently but individual operations are fast
- Use `fixed = TRUE` in `strsplit` if no regex patterns needed
- Cache pattern to avoid repeated creation

## Parent Issue

This is part of the comprehensive performance optimization analysis in #1765

title: Use vapply for path extraction in simulation setup
Ensure consistent pre-allocation of vectors in loops
body:
## Issue Overview

Ensure consistent pre-allocation of vectors and lists in loops across the codebase to avoid repeated memory allocations.

## Current Problem

**Multiple Files**

```r
# Good pattern: pre-allocated
paths <- vector("character", length(entitiesOrPaths))
for (idx in seq_along(entitiesOrPaths)) {
  paths[[idx]] <- ...  # Good: pre-allocated
}

# But also patterns like:
results <- list()
for (i in seq_along(items)) {
  results[[i]] <- processItem(items[[i]])  # Growing list
}
```

**Issues**:
- Some loops properly pre-allocate, others don't
- Growing lists can cause repeated memory allocations
- Inconsistent patterns across codebase

## Proposed Solution

Always pre-allocate when size is known:

```r
# Always pre-allocate when size is known
results <- vector("list", length(items))
for (i in seq_along(items)) {
  results[[i]] <- processItem(items[[i]])
}

# For data frames
nRows <- length(individualIds) * valueLength
df <- data.frame(
  col1 = vector("type", nRows),
  col2 = vector("type", nRows),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)
```

## Expected Impact

- **Priority**: LOW-MEDIUM
- **Estimated Impact**: 10-20% reduction in memory churn
- **Effort**: Low
- **Testing**: Review loops throughout codebase and add pre-allocation where missing

## Implementation Notes

- Depends on loop size
- Scan codebase for growing vector/list patterns
- Establish coding standard for pre-allocation

## Parent Issue

This is part of the comprehensive performance optimization analysis in #1765

title: Optimize cache printing for large caches
body:
## Issue Overview

Optimize the cache printing function to avoid multiple I/O operations when printing large caches.

## Current Problem

**File**: `R/cache.R:84-89`

```r
print = function(...) {
  cat("Cache for objects of type: ", self$type, "\n", sep = "")
  cat("Cached keys:\n")
  lapply(self$keys, function(key) {
    cat(key, "\n", sep = "")  # I/O operation per key
  })
  invisible(self)
}
```

**Issues**:
- I/O operation in loop
- For cache with 10,000 keys = 10,000+ `cat` calls
- Inefficient output buffering

## Proposed Solution

Single concatenated output:

```r
print = function(...) {
  cat("Cache for objects of type: ", self$type, "\n", sep = "")
  cat("Cached keys:\n")

  # Single concatenated output
  if (length(self$keys) > 0) {
    cat(paste(self$keys, collapse = "\n"), "\n", sep = "")
  }

  invisible(self)
}
```

## Expected Impact

- **Priority**: LOW
- **Estimated Impact**: Negligible runtime impact, improves debugging experience
- **Effort**: Low
- **Testing**: Test cache printing with small and large caches

## Implementation Notes

- Only affects debugging/printing, not runtime performance
- Good for improving developer experience
- Low priority but easy to implement

## Parent Issue

This is part of the comprehensive performance optimization analysis in #1765
